Block 5 · Week 12 LO 43E — Cochlear & Vestibular Microstructure Histology · Function · Congenital Dysfunction
Overview External & Middle Ear Inner Ear Structure Cochlea Histology Organ of Corti Sound Transduction Vestibular System Hair Cell Physiology Congenital Dysfunction Gallery
LO 43EAcoustico-Vestibular Histology & Physiology

The Ear: Overview

The ear is divided into three anatomical compartments — external, middle, and inner — that together convert mechanical sound vibrations into neural signals, and also detect head position and movement for balance.

External Ear→ (auricle + EAM) → Middle Ear → (ossicles amplify ×20) → Inner Ear → (cochlea + vestibular apparatus) → CN VIII → Brain
🎧 The Two Functions
The inner ear serves two completely distinct roles: hearing (cochlear apparatus → cochlear branch of CN VIII) and balance (vestibular apparatus → vestibular branch of CN VIII). Both share the same fluid compartments.
Brödel 1939 Classic ear anatomy — external canal → drum → ossicles → cochlea → CN VIII
Overview External, middle, and inner ear with CN VII and CN VIII labeled
System Overview The acoustico-vestibular system — balance, head/neck/eye coordination

External & Middle Ear

External Ear

The auricle (pinna) is composed of elastic cartilage covered by skin, with sebaceous glands and hair follicles. It funnels sound into the external acoustic meatus (EAM), which is lined by skin with ceruminous glands (modified apocrine glands) in its outer cartilaginous third and is largely bony in its inner two-thirds.

Tympanic Membrane

The eardrum is a three-layered structure:

LayerCompositionNotes
Outer (lateral)Stratified squamous epithelium (skin)Continuous with EAM lining
MiddleFibrous connective tissueRadial + circular collagen fibers; absent in pars flaccida
Inner (medial)Simple squamous / cuboidal epitheliumContinuous with middle ear mucosa
📌 Pars Flaccida vs Pars Tensa
The pars flaccida (Shrapnell's membrane) — superior to the malleolar folds — lacks the middle fibrous layer, making it flaccid. Cholesteatomas most commonly originate here. The pars tensa has all three layers and bears the umbo (lowest point of the malleus handle) and the cone of light (anterior-inferior reflex on otoscopy).
Otoscopic ViewNormal tympanic membrane — note light reflex (cone of light) anterior-inferiorly
TM AnatomyPars flaccida, pars tensa, umbo, manubrium, annulus, cone of light

Middle Ear (Tympanic Cavity)

An air-filled space within the petrous temporal bone, lined by simple cuboidal to squamous epithelium (ciliated columnar near the auditory tube). It contains the three auditory ossicles:

🔨
Malleus
Hammer
Handle (manubrium) fused to TM; head articulates with incus
🔗
Incus
Anvil
Bridge between malleus and stapes
🔵
Stapes
Stirrup
Footplate sits in oval window; transmits vibration to perilymph
💪
Stapedius
Stapes Muscle
CN VII; dampens loud sounds; paralysis → hyperacusis
💪
Tensor Tympani
Malleus Muscle
CN V3; pulls malleus medially; stiffens TM
🌬️
Auditory Tube
Eustachian Tube
Equalizes pressure; connects to nasopharynx
⚠️ Amplification Mechanism
The ossicular chain amplifies sound pressure by ~20-fold via two mechanisms: (1) the TM is ~17× larger than the oval window footplate (area ratio), and (2) the lever action of the ossicular chain (incus arm longer than malleus arm). This overcomes the air-to-fluid impedance mismatch at the oval window.
Middle Ear 1Malleus, incus, stapes; stapedius; tensor tympani; oval & round windows; auditory tube
Middle Ear 2Stapedius, tensor tympani (cut), round window, auditory tube; tympanic cavity

Inner Ear: The Labyrinth

The inner ear consists of a bony labyrinth (cavities in the petrous temporal bone) encasing a membranous labyrinth (epithelial tubes and sacs within).

Bony Labyrinth

Three bony compartments filled with perilymph (similar to ECF — high Na⁺, low K⁺):

  • Vestibule — central chamber; contains oval and round windows; houses utricle and saccule
  • Semicircular canals — 3 canals (anterior, posterior, lateral/horizontal), each at 90° to the others; each has an ampulla containing the crista
  • Cochlea — 2½ turns around the central modiolus (bony core containing spiral ganglion and cochlear nerve)

Membranous Labyrinth

A closed system of epithelial ducts filled with endolymph (similar to ICF — high K⁺, low Na⁺; maintained by stria vascularis). It is suspended in perilymph within the bony labyrinth.

FluidLocationCompositionProduced by
PerilymphScala vestibuli, scala tympani, bony labyrinthHigh Na⁺ (~140 mEq/L), Low K⁺ (~5 mEq/L)Filtrate from blood / CSF
EndolymphScala media (cochlear duct), membranous labyrinthHigh K⁺ (~150 mEq/L), Low Na⁺ (~1 mEq/L)Stria vascularis (cochlea); dark cells (vestibular)
🔑 Endolymph = Unique Fluid
Endolymph is the only extracellular fluid in the body with an ionic composition resembling intracellular fluid (high K⁺). This creates an endocochlear potential of +80 mV (relative to perilymph), which is the driving force for K⁺ influx into hair cells during mechanotransduction. Loss of this potential → sensorineural hearing loss.
Labyrinth ComparisonBony labyrinth (left) vs membranous labyrinth within it (right) — saccule, utricle, cochlear duct, semicircular ducts
Membranous LabyrinthUnrolled cochlea showing scala vestibuli (perilymph), scala media/cochlear duct (endolymph), scala tympani (perilymph)
Vestibular ApparatusPosition of cochlea, semicircular canals, CN VIII within the temporal bone; internal acoustic meatus

Cochlea Histology

The cochlea spirals 2½ turns around the modiolus. Each turn, when cross-sectioned, reveals three fluid-filled compartments (scala) separated by two membranes.

The Three Scala

CompartmentFluidBoundariesNotes
Scala VestibuliPerilymphAbove vestibular (Reissner's) membraneBegins at oval window; connected to scala tympani at helicotrema (apex)
Scala Media (cochlear duct)EndolymphBetween Reissner's membrane and basilar membraneMembranous labyrinth proper; contains organ of Corti; lateral wall = stria vascularis
Scala TympaniPerilymphBelow basilar membraneEnds at round window (secondary tympanic membrane)

Key Cochlear Structures

Modiolus

Central bony pillar of the cochlea. Contains the spiral ganglion (bipolar neurons — cell bodies of the cochlear nerve) and the central fibers of the cochlear branch of CN VIII.

Spiral Lamina

Bony shelf projecting from the modiolus. Contains peripheral nerve fibers passing from the spiral ganglion to the hair cells. The point where these fibers cross from bone to the basilar membrane is the habenula perforata.

Spiral Ligament

Thickened periosteum on the lateral cochlear wall. Anchors the basilar membrane laterally. Contains fibrocytes that help maintain the endocochlear potential via ion recycling (K⁺ return pathway).

Vestibular (Reissner's) Membrane

Two cell layers thick: simple squamous epithelium facing scala vestibuli and scala media. Separates perilymph from endolymph. Plays a role in endolymph homeostasis.

Basilar Membrane

Separates scala media from scala tympani. Supports the organ of Corti. Key property: tonotopic gradient — narrow and stiff at the base (high frequency, ~20 kHz) → wide and floppy at the apex (low frequency, ~20 Hz). This underlies frequency analysis.

Stria Vascularis

Specialized vascular epithelium on the lateral wall of scala media. Contains 3 cell types: marginal cells (secrete K⁺ into endolymph), intermediate cells, and basal cells. The only epithelium in the body with intraepithelial capillaries. Primary producer of endolymph and the endocochlear potential.

🔬 Spiral Limbus
The spiral limbus is a ridge of connective tissue on the modiolar side, from which the tectorial membrane originates. Interdental cells on the limbus secrete the tectorial membrane. Border cells separate the limbus from the inner hair cells.
Fig 5 — Basal Turn Cross-SectionThree scala, stria vascularis, spiral ligament, spiral lamina, spiral ganglion, auditory nerve (H&E)
Cross-SectionsMultiple turns showing modiolus, scala vestibuli, cochlear duct, scala tympani, spiral ganglion, CN VIII
Whole CochleaHelicotrema at apex, modiolus, stria vascularis, auditory spinal ganglion, internal acoustic meatus
Fig 8 — Walls DetailStria vascularis, spiral ligament, basilar membrane, tectorial membrane, habenula perforata, spiral ganglion
Cochlear Duct DetailVestibular membrane, tectorial membrane, basilar membrane, organ of Corti, spiral ganglion, cochlear branch CN VIII
Fig 9 — Scala MediaOrgan of Corti, spiral limbus, Reissner's membrane (vestibular membrane), stria vascularis

Organ of Corti

The organ of Corti (spiral organ) rests on the basilar membrane within the scala media. It is the sensory epithelium for hearing — containing ~16,000 mechanosensory hair cells arranged along the 35 mm length of the cochlea.

Hair Cells

FeatureInner Hair Cells (IHC)Outer Hair Cells (OHC)
Number~3,500 (single row)~12,000 (3 rows)
ShapeFlask-shapedCylindrical
LocationModiolar side of tunnel of CortiLateral side of tunnel of Corti
Afferent innervation95% of afferent fibers (CN VIII cochlear)5% of afferent fibers
Efferent innervationFew efferentsMostly efferents from superior olivary complex
Primary functionSensory transduction (signal to brain)Cochlear amplification (electromotility)
Stereocilia~50–70, straight rows~100–150, V or W pattern
Tectorial membrane contactIndirect (tip links in endolymph)Stereocilia tips embed in tectorial membrane
⚠️ Clinical Pearl — OHC Loss First
Outer hair cells are far more vulnerable to noise damage, ototoxic drugs (aminoglycosides, loop diuretics), and aging. OHC loss → reduced cochlear amplification → high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL). IHC loss (the actual signal transducers) causes profound deafness. Because IHC send 95% of auditory information, they are the targets for cochlear implant electrode stimulation.

Supporting Cells

Cell TypeLocationFunction
Inner Pillar CellsModiolar side of tunnelForm inner wall of tunnel of Corti; structural support
Outer Pillar CellsLateral side of tunnelForm outer wall; together with inner pillars create rigid triangular tunnel
Inner Phalangeal CellsCup the base of IHCsMechanical support; separate IHCs
Outer Phalangeal (Deiters') CellsCup the base of OHCsPhalangeal projections extend apically to reticular lamina
Cells of HensenLateral to OHCsSupporting; possible role in K⁺ recycling
Cells of ClaudiusFurther lateralColumnar epithelium; ion transport
Cells of BoettcherBelow cells of Claudius (basal turns)Secretory; produce material for scala media
Border CellsInner edge, medial to IHCMark boundary between limbus and IHC region
Interdental CellsOn spiral limbusSecrete tectorial membrane

Key Membranes of the Organ of Corti

Tectorial Membrane

Acellular gelatinous structure secreted by interdental cells of the spiral limbus. Composed of collagen fibers and proteoglycans. OHC stereocilia tips are embedded in its undersurface. IHC stereocilia are deflected by the shear motion between basilar and tectorial membranes. Mutations in tectorial membrane proteins (e.g., α-tectorin/TECTA) cause autosomal dominant SNHL.

Reticular Lamina

The tight-junction-sealed apical surface formed by phalangeal processes of Deiters' cells and the apical surfaces of hair cells. It forms the barrier between endolymph (above) and perilymph (below) at the level of the hair cell cuticular plate. This seal is critical for maintaining the electrochemical gradient that drives transduction.

🏛️ Tunnel of Corti
The triangular space between inner and outer pillar cells is the tunnel of Corti. It is filled with cortilymph (similar to perilymph). Radial nerve fibers cross through the tunnel to reach the OHCs. The outer tunnel (space of Nuel) lies between OHC rows and Deiters' cells.
Organ of Corti DiagramIHC, OHC, pillar cells, phalangeal cells, stereocilia, tectorial membrane, scala tympani — ~16,000 hair cells total
Fig 11 — OC HistologyCells of Claudius, Hensen, Boettcher; OHC & IHC; pillar cells; outer & inner tunnel; reticular lamina
Fig 15 — High MagOHC stereocilia contacting tectorial membrane, tunnel of Corti, vestibular lip, internal spiral tunnel
Unrolled CochleaBase (oval window) → apex (helicotrema); basilar membrane tonotopy

Sound Transduction & Tonotopy

The Full Pathway

① Sound waves → TM vibrates → ② Ossicles amplify → Stapes footplate pushes oval window → ③ Pressure waves in scala vestibuli (perilymph) → ④ Distorts vestibular membrane → pressure in endolymph → basilar membrane deflects → ⑤ OHC stereocilia shear against tectorial membrane → K⁺ influx → depolarization → CN VIII signal → ⑥ Remaining wave passes through helicotrema to scala tympani → exits via round window

Mechanotransduction at the Hair Cell

Stereocilia are arranged in a staircase pattern from shortest to tallest. Tip links — fine filaments of cadherin-23 and protocadherin-15 — connect the tip of each shorter stereocilium to the side of its taller neighbor. When the stereocilia bundle deflects toward the tallest stereocilium:

  • Tip links are placed under tension, mechanically gating MET (mechanosensitive) channels (MYO7A, TMC1/2)
  • K⁺ (and Ca²⁺) flood into the cell from the high-K⁺ endolymph
  • Depolarization triggers glutamate release at the afferent synapse → CN VIII fires

Deflection away from the tallest stereocilium closes channels → hyperpolarization → reduced firing rate. This push-pull mechanism allows detection of direction and frequency.

🧬 Molecular Basis — Connexin & Gap Junctions
After K⁺ enters the hair cell, it must be recycled back to the stria vascularis to regenerate endolymph. This occurs via gap junctions (connexin 26/GJB2 and connexin 30/GJB6) in the supporting cells and fibrocytes of the spiral ligament. Mutations in GJB2 (connexin 26) are the most common cause of autosomal recessive congenital sensorineural deafness (DFNB1), accounting for ~50% of non-syndromic hereditary deafness in many populations.

Tonotopy and the Basilar Membrane

The basilar membrane acts as a frequency analyzer due to its graded mechanical properties:

LocationBasilar MembraneFrequency CodedClinical Relevance
Base (near oval/round windows)Narrow, thick, stiffHigh frequencies (8–20 kHz)First affected in noise-induced SNHL (4 kHz notch)
Apex (helicotrema)Wide, thin, floppyLow frequencies (20–500 Hz)Preserved longest in presbycusis and noise damage
Sound TransductionComplete pathway — numbered steps 1–5 from TM to round window
Straightened LabyrinthSteps 1–7 in context of cochlear anatomy — stapes → oval window → helicotrema → round window
Tonotopic MappingCochlear implant electrode placement — apex = low frequency, base = high frequency

Vestibular System Histology

The vestibular apparatus detects two types of motion: angular acceleration (semicircular canals → crista ampullaris) and linear acceleration / head tilt / gravity (utricle and saccule → maculae).

Semicircular Canals & Crista Ampullaris

Each of the three semicircular ducts (within the bony canals) has an enlarged ampulla at one end. The sensory organ within the ampulla is the crista ampullaris:

StructureComposition / DescriptionFunction
Crista ampullarisRidge of neuroepithelium on a connective tissue core; projects into the endolymphThe sensory organ proper
CupulaGelatinous glycoprotein mass sitting atop the crista; extends to the roof of the ampullaDeflects with endolymph flow; moves stereocilia
Hair cells (Type I & II)Embedded in neuroepithelium; surrounded by supporting cellsMechanosensory transducers
Vestibular nerve fibersDense fibers in the core of the crista (visible as dark streaks in H&E)Afferent fibers → superior vestibular nerve (CN VIII)
💡 How the Crista Works
When the head rotates, the bony canal moves with the head, but endolymph lags behind due to inertia. This relative flow bends the cupula, deflecting the hair cell stereocilia. Deflection toward the kinocilium (longest cilium) = depolarization = increased firing. The canal ipsilateral to the direction of rotation is excited; the contralateral canal is inhibited — the brain computes rotation from this push-pull signal.

Orientation of the Semicircular Canals

  • Lateral (horizontal) canal: Tilted 30° above horizontal; detects horizontal (yaw) rotation
  • Anterior (superior) canal: In the sagittal plane; detects pitch (head nodding) and roll
  • Posterior canal: In the coronal plane; detects roll; contralateral pair with the opposite ear's anterior canal

The three canals are oriented at approximately 90° to each other, giving complete three-dimensional coverage of rotational motion.

Canal OrientationThree canals each at 90°; horizontal canal tilted 30°; paired with contralateral canals
Vestibular ApparatusAnterior, posterior, horizontal semicircular canals; utricle; sacculus
Crista AmpullarisAmpullary crest, cupula, hair cells, neuroepithelium, superior vestibular nerve fibers
Crista — High MagNeuroepithelium structure; cupula at top; dense hair cell layer
Crista Full ViewCupula, neuroepithelium, vestibular nerve fibers, perilymphatic space, membranous labyrinth within osseous labyrinth
Fig 20 — Temporal BoneCochlea, vestibular nerve, stapedius muscle, facial nerve (CN VII), saccule, utricle, ampulla — whole section

The Otolith Organs: Utricle & Saccule

These two sacs in the vestibule detect linear acceleration and head tilt relative to gravity. Their sensory organ is the macula (plural: maculae):

FeatureMacula of UtricleMacula of Saccule
PlaneHorizontal (floor of utricle)Vertical (medial wall of saccule)
Motion detectedHorizontal linear acceleration; forward/backward tiltVertical linear acceleration (up/down; e.g., elevator)
NerveUtricular nerve (superior vestibular nerve, CN VIII)Saccular nerve (inferior vestibular nerve, CN VIII)
StriolaCurved dividing line; hair cells on opposite sides have opposite polaritySimilar striola; allows bidirectional detection

Macula Microstructure (Layers, deep to superficial)

Vestibular nerve fibers + lamina propria → Hair cells + supporting cells → Stereocilia & kinocilia → Otolithic membrane (gelatinous layer) → Otoliths (otoconia) CaCO₃ crystals; density > endolymph
⚖️ How the Macula Works
The otoliths (calcium carbonate crystals embedded in the gelatinous otolithic membrane) are denser than endolymph. Gravity or linear acceleration causes the otolithic membrane to slide relative to the hair cells below. This shear bends the stereocilia → mechanotransduction → vestibular nerve signal → brain interprets head position relative to gravity.

BPPV (Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo): Otoliths become dislodged from the macula and fall into the semicircular canal (usually posterior canal) — cupulolithiasis or canalolithiasis. Head movement causes abnormal cupula deflection → brief intense vertigo.
Macula of UtricleOtolith layer (dark), stereocilia, hair cells, vestibular nerve fibers; endolymph above, perilymph below
Macula High MagLayer by layer: nerve fibers → hair cells + support cells → stereocilia/kinocilia → otoliths
Hair Cell Types I & IIType I (HCI) flask-shaped, type II (HCII) cylindrical; kinocilium (KC), stereocilia (SC), otoconia (OC), basement membrane (BM)
Fig 21 — VestibuleMacula of saccule, macula of utricle, crista of lateral ampulla — E=endolymph, P=perilymph

Vestibular & Cochlear Hair Cell Physiology

Type I vs Type II Vestibular Hair Cells

FeatureType I Hair CellType II Hair Cell
ShapeFlask / goblet (rounded base)Cylindrical (straight sides)
Afferent synapseLarge calyx-type ending encups entire cell bodyBouton-type endings at base
LocationCenter of neuroepithelium (more concentrated in crista summit)Periphery of neuroepithelium
AnalogySimilar to cochlear IHC (primary transducer)Similar to cochlear OHC

Mechanotransduction — The Tip Link Model

All hair cells (cochlear and vestibular) share the same basic transduction mechanism:

  • Each stereocilium has tip links — thin filaments connecting to the next taller stereocilium
  • Deflection toward the tallest → tip links pull open MET channels → K⁺ (and Ca²⁺) flood in from endolymph → depolarization → glutamate release → afferent firing ↑
  • Deflection away from the tallest → tip links slacken → channels close → hyperpolarization → afferent firing ↓
  • This creates a push-pull system with the contralateral canal's hair cells bending the opposite direction simultaneously
👁️ Vestibulo-Ocular Reflex (VOR)
The VOR stabilizes gaze during head movement. When you turn your head to the right:
→ Right horizontal canal excited (endolymph lags, bends cupula) → right vestibular nucleus excited → left abducens nucleus excited → left lateral rectus contracts and right medial rectus contracts → eyes move LEFT to compensate.

The ear you're turning toward = side of increased activity. The abducens nucleus on the opposite side drives the compensatory eye movement. This is why with nystagmus, the fast phase beats toward the healthy side (away from a damaged labyrinth).

K⁺ Recycling in the Cochlea

K⁺ enters hair cell via MET channels → K⁺ exits via basolateral channels (KCNQ4) → K⁺ taken up by Deiters' cells (supporting cells) → K⁺ passes through gap junctions (Cx26/Cx30) in fibrocytes → K⁺ secreted back into endolymph by stria vascularis (completing the circuit)

Congenital Dysfunction

⚠️ Key Concept
~1–3 per 1,000 newborns have significant sensorineural hearing loss. ~50% of cases have a genetic cause. The cochlea is particularly vulnerable because its hair cells are terminally differentiated — mammals cannot regenerate cochlear hair cells. Understanding the microstructure predicts the pathophysiology.
ConditionGene / CauseStructural DefectResult
DFNB1 (most common AR SNHL)GJB2 (Connexin 26) / GJB6 (Cx30)Gap junction dysfunction → K⁺ recycling failure → endolymph K⁺ dropsSevere-to-profound bilateral SNHL; non-syndromic
Usher Syndrome Type IMYO7A (myosin VIIa), CDH23, PCDH15, etc.Defective tip links or stereocilia cohesion → failed mechanotransduction in cochlea AND retinaProfound congenital SNHL + retinitis pigmentosa; vestibular dysfunction
Pendred SyndromeSLC26A4 (pendrin)Pendrin is an anion transporter in stria vascularis and thyroid; enlarged vestibular aqueduct (EVA)Bilateral SNHL (often fluctuating) + goiter; Mondini cochlea deformity may be present
Waardenburg SyndromePAX3, MITF, SOX10, EDN3, EDNRBAbsence of melanocytes in stria vascularis (melanocytes required for endocochlear potential); pigmentation defectsCongenital SNHL, white forelock, heterochromia iridis, dystopia canthorum
CHARGE SyndromeCHD7 (chromodomain helicase)Aplasia/hypoplasia of semicircular canals (most consistent inner ear finding); cochlear defectsColoboma, Heart, choanal Atresia, Retardation, Genital, Ear anomalies; vestibular dysfunction → delayed walking
Treacher CollinsTCOF1 (treacle)Neural crest migration failure → hypoplastic ossicles; EAC atresia; malformed auricleConductive hearing loss (CHL); bilateral, severe
Ototoxicity (aminoglycosides)MTRNR1 (12S rRNA) mutation increases susceptibilityOHC apoptosis beginning at base → progressive SNHL; stria vascularis damageHigh-frequency SNHL; partially reversible with cessation
Ménière's DiseaseEndolymphatic hydrops (idiopathic / multifactorial)Endolymph overproduction or impaired resorption → dilation of membranous labyrinth → Reissner's membrane ruptureEpisodic SNHL + tinnitus + vertigo + aural fullness
BPPVOtolith displacement (idiopathic, trauma, aging)Otoconia detach from utricular macula → enter semicircular canal (usually posterior) → cupulolithiasisBrief positional vertigo (Dix-Hallpike positive); treated with Epley maneuver

Cochlear Implants — Bridging Histology to Treatment

Cochlear implants work when hair cells are destroyed but the spiral ganglion neurons remain intact. The electrode array is inserted into the scala tympani (largest, most accessible) through the round window or cochleostomy. Electrodes stimulate spiral ganglion cell bodies (or peripheral processes) directly, bypassing the hair cells entirely.

Cochlear Implant FactorHistological Basis
Electrode goes in scala tympaniLargest perilymph-filled space; close to spiral ganglion; least surgical risk to cochlear duct
Tonotopic electrode placementBase of cochlea = high freq; apex = low freq; multiple electrodes placed along gradient for frequency discrimination
Best outcomes with early implantationAuditory cortex plasticity window; spiral ganglion neuron survival higher before degeneration from disuse
Contraindicated if spiral ganglion destroyedNo neurons to stimulate → auditory brainstem implant (ABI) required instead
Tonotopic MappingCochlear implant electrode targeting — apex = low frequency, base = high frequency

Self-Check MCQs

Seven high-yield review questions for cochlear and vestibular microstructure. Distractors are kept close in length so the answer is not visually telegraphed.

1. Which structure generates the potassium-rich endolymph and endocochlear potential required for cochlear transduction?
  • A. Stria vascularis, lining the lateral wall of the scala media
  • B. Spiral ligament, anchoring the basilar membrane laterally
  • C. Tectorial membrane, overhanging the stereocilia of hair cells
  • D. Modiolus, housing the central axis of the cochlear spiral
Reveal Answer

A. The stria vascularis actively secretes K⁺ into endolymph and establishes the endocochlear potential.

2. Which cell population serves as the primary sensory receptor population for hearing?
  • A. Inner hair cells, which receive most afferent auditory innervation
  • B. Outer hair cells, which receive most afferent auditory innervation
  • C. Pillar cells, which receive most afferent auditory innervation
  • D. Hensen cells, which receive most afferent auditory innervation
Reveal Answer

A. Inner hair cells are the main sensory receptors and account for the vast majority of afferent input to the auditory nerve.

3. Otoconia are most directly associated with which vestibular sensory region?
  • A. Maculae of utricle and saccule, which detect linear acceleration
  • B. Cristae of ampullae, which detect linear acceleration exclusively
  • C. Organ of Corti, which detects angular acceleration in the cochlea
  • D. Spiral limbus, which detects angular acceleration near the modiolus
Reveal Answer

A. Otoconia sit on the otolithic membrane over the maculae of the utricle and saccule, helping detect gravity and linear acceleration.

4. The crista ampullaris is specialized to detect which type of head movement?
  • A. Angular acceleration during rotational movements of the head
  • B. Linear acceleration during translational movements of the head
  • C. Static pressure changes during middle ear equalization only
  • D. High-frequency sound vibrations during cochlear amplification only
Reveal Answer

A. The crista ampullaris in each semicircular duct detects angular acceleration via cupular deflection.

5. Why does a Connexin 26 defect commonly produce severe congenital sensorineural hearing loss?
  • A. Potassium recycling fails, disrupting endolymph homeostasis and hair-cell function
  • B. Otoconia calcify early, disrupting endolymph homeostasis and hair-cell function
  • C. Ossicles fuse early, disrupting endolymph homeostasis and hair-cell function
  • D. Semicircular ducts collapse, disrupting endolymph homeostasis and hair-cell function
Reveal Answer

A. Connexin defects impair gap-junction-mediated potassium recycling, which is essential for normal cochlear ionic physiology.

6. Which statement best explains why cochlear implants can restore hearing in some profoundly deaf patients?
  • A. They bypass damaged hair cells and directly stimulate spiral ganglion neurons
  • B. They regenerate damaged hair cells and directly stimulate spiral ganglion neurons
  • C. They bypass damaged ossicles and directly stimulate spiral ganglion neurons
  • D. They regenerate damaged otoconia and directly stimulate spiral ganglion neurons
Reveal Answer

A. Cochlear implants work by bypassing lost hair-cell transduction and electrically activating surviving spiral ganglion neurons.

7. Which congenital syndrome is most strongly associated with absent melanocytes in the stria vascularis?
  • A. Waardenburg syndrome, producing pigment changes and congenital hearing loss
  • B. Pendred syndrome, producing pigment changes and congenital hearing loss
  • C. Usher syndrome, producing pigment changes and congenital hearing loss
  • D. CHARGE syndrome, producing pigment changes and congenital hearing loss
Reveal Answer

A. Waardenburg syndrome involves melanocyte abnormalities, including loss in the stria vascularis, which disrupts the endocochlear potential.
